content.full_text:
October 21, 1991


Ann Bailey, Chief Counsel
Senate Legislative Ethics Committee
State Capitol, Room 412
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. I-91-424

Dear Ms. Bailey:

You have requested advice concerning the application of the
gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because
your request seeks general guidance specific to no particular
individual, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.²

QUESTION

Are parking passes given to a state legislator by airports
and used for the legislator's official business reportable gifts
to the legislator?

CONCLUSION

Parking passes given to a state legislator by airports and
used for the legislator's official business are reportable gifts
to the legislator.

ANALYSIS

Your request for advice seeks interpretation of a Commission
opinion, In re Thomas, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 30. In Thomas, a state
agency provided a free parking pass to a state legislator. Thomas
held that the pass was not a gift. The Commission reasoned that

_________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the government code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

the provision of free parking by a state agency to a state
official for state business was akin to a governmental employer's
payment of expenses to its employee, which are exempted under the
Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).)

The exception in Thomas is an extremely narrow one. Its
reasoning arises from the concept that compensation from one's own
agency generally does not constitute an economic interest of the
official and does not cause a conflict of interest. The situation
you present is different. While the free parking would be
provided by a governmental entity, the airport, the entity is
different from that of the official's. Thomas and the Glad Advice
Letter (No. I-90-148, cited in your request) do not hold that any
pass provided by any governmental entity for the purposes of any
official's official business are not gifts.3 Moreover, we note
that under the facts in Thomas, 100 percent of the official's time
was to be spent on official business. The legislator in Thomas
received a free parking pass solely to attend a meeting at the
museum, no more. In contrast, a legislator may not spend all of
his or her time in Sacramento on official business, and allocating
how much of his or her time is official or personal would be
difficult.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Mark T. Morodomi
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MM

3     To the extent, if any, the Glad Letter is inconsistent with
this letter, this letter supersedes.
